title: Women and families in classical societies
description: Test your knowledge about women's lives in Imperial Rome and Han China!
topic_path: Women and families in classical societies

hints: Kinship includes the relationship between parents and children, but that's not all it covers!

choices.content: the relationship between parents and children
the relationship between husband and wife
a broad term that includes blood relations and relationships by marriage

assessment_items.question_content:
**In which religious tradition did women have the opportunity to be members of religious orders?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Women did not have the opportunity to be part of Confucian religious orders.

choices.content: Confucianism
Daoism
Hinduism

assessment_items.question_content:
**In Imperial Rome and Han China, who had legal control of women’s lives?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Women's lives were not controlled by female kin.

choices.content: male kin
female kin
themselves

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question, read this excerpt from female Chinese scholar Ban Zhao’s book _Lessons for Women:_

> "Let a woman yield modestly to others; let her respect others; let her put others first. Herself, last.


> Lay the girl baby (at birth) below the bed plainly to indicate that she is lowly and weak, and should regard it as her primary duty to humble herself before others.

> A husband can marry twice, but his wife must never remarry. Just as Heaven cannot be disobeyed, so the wife cannot keep away from her husband.

> Man is honored for strength; a woman is beautiful on account of her gentleness."

**Which of Ban Zhao’s statements sound most similar to ideas about Imperial Roman society?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: There was no rule stating that Imperial Roman men needed to marry more than once.

choices.content: Women should yield to men
Men must marry twice
A baby girl should be laid below the bed
Wives shouldn’t ever be away from their husbands

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question, read this excerpt from female Chinese scholar Ban Zhao’s book _Lessons for Women:_


> "Let a woman yield modestly to others; let her respect others; let her put others first. Herself, last.


> Lay the girl baby (at birth) below the bed plainly to indicate that she is lowly and weak, and should regard it as her primary duty to humble herself before others.

> A husband can marry twice, but his wife must never remarry. Just as Heaven cannot be disobeyed, so the wife cannot keep away from her husband.

> Man is honored for strength; a woman is beautiful on account of her gentleness."

**Based on Ban Zhao’s writings, what is one possible difference between her words and the way women were actually treated?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about Ban Zhao, a female writer who was able to publish her own work under her own name during the Han dynasty.

choices.content: Ban Zhao says women should be submissive, but she had enough power to write and publish a book.
Ban Zhao says women are “beautiful on account of [their] gentleness,” and Daoism praises the female “yin” of submissiveness.
She says a wife must never remarry, but women remarried often in Han Chinese society and faced no penalty for it.
She says women must put their husbands before themselves, but men often had to answer to their wives when making decisions that affected the household.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the questions that follow. 
 
>It is clear, however, that Roman women in general had much greater independence than women in most parts of the classical Greek or Near Eastern worlds, limited as it must seem in modern terms. The contrast is particularly striking with classical Athens, where women of wealthy families were supposed to live secluded lives, out of the public eye, largely segregated from men and male social life (the poor, needless to say, did not have the cash or the space to enforce any such divisions). There were, to be sure, uncomfortable restrictions on women in Rome, too: the emperor Augustus, for example, relegated them to the back rows of the theatres and gladiatorial arenas; the suites for women in  public baths were usually markedly more cramped than those for men; and in practice male activities probably dominated the swankier areas of a Roman house. But women were not meant to be publicly invisible, and domestic life does not seem to have been formally divided into male and female spaces, with gendered no-go areas. 
 
From: Mary Beard, SPQR: A History of Ancient Rome (New York: Liveright Publishing Corporation, 2015), 307. 

**What is Beard’s main claim about the independence of Roman women in the classical period?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Compared to any standard, Roman women had very little independence
Compared to modern women, Roman women had less independence
Compared to most Greek and Near Eastern women, Roman women had more independence
Compared to Roman men, Roman women had nearly equal rights

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the questions that follow. 
 
>It is clear, however, that Roman women in general had much greater independence than women in most parts of the classical Greek or Near Eastern worlds, limited as it must seem in modern terms. The contrast is particularly striking with classical Athens, where women of wealthy families were supposed to live secluded lives, out of the public eye, largely segregated from men and male social life (the poor, needless to say, did not have the cash or the space to enforce any such divisions). There were, to be sure, uncomfortable restrictions on women in Rome, too: the emperor Augustus, for example, relegated them to the back rows of the theatres and gladiatorial arenas; the suites for women in  public baths were usually markedly more cramped than those for men; and in practice male activities probably dominated the swankier areas of a Roman house. But women were not meant to be publicly invisible, and domestic life does not seem to have been formally divided into male and female spaces, with gendered no-go areas. 
 
From: Mary Beard, SPQR: A History of Ancient Rome (New York: Liveright Publishing Corporation, 2015), 307. 

**Beard notes that the independence of Roman women will seem limited when compared to modern life. Which of the following could explain why she does not go on to use modern life as a point of comparison?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: This point is meant to remind the reader that we must view historical events in their own contexts
Roman society is so far removed from our modern experience that it would be impossible to say what actually caused any differences or similarities we observe
She made a poor choice; she should have compared Roman society to our modern society because it is more relatable to readers
Historical comparisons have to be between things that occurred at the same time

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the questions that follow. 
 
>It is clear, however, that Roman women in general had much greater independence than women in most parts of the classical Greek or Near Eastern worlds, limited as it must seem in modern terms. The contrast is particularly striking with classical Athens, where women of wealthy families were supposed to live secluded lives, out of the public eye, largely segregated from men and male social life (the poor, needless to say, did not have the cash or the space to enforce any such divisions). There were, to be sure, uncomfortable restrictions on women in Rome, too: the emperor Augustus, for example, relegated them to the back rows of the theatres and gladiatorial arenas; the suites for women in  public baths were usually markedly more cramped than those for men; and in practice male activities probably dominated the swankier areas of a Roman house. But women were not meant to be publicly invisible, and domestic life does not seem to have been formally divided into male and female spaces, with gendered no-go areas. 
 
From: Mary Beard, SPQR: A History of Ancient Rome (New York: Liveright Publishing Corporation, 2015), 307. 

**How does Beard use women in Athens as evidence for her claim?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Beard simply states that they were less independent than Roman women
Beard shows what life was like for Roman women.
Beard offers clear comparisons between restrictions on women in Athens and Rome
Beard explains how Athenian women felt about these restrictions on them

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the questions that follow. 
 
>It is clear, however, that Roman women in general had much greater independence than women in most parts of the classical Greek or Near Eastern worlds, limited as it must seem in modern terms. The contrast is particularly striking with classical Athens, where women of wealthy families were supposed to live secluded lives, out of the public eye, largely segregated from men and male social life (the poor, needless to say, did not have the cash or the space to enforce any such divisions). There were, to be sure, uncomfortable restrictions on women in Rome, too: the emperor Augustus, for example, relegated them to the back rows of the theatres and gladiatorial arenas; the suites for women in  public baths were usually markedly more cramped than those for men; and in practice male activities probably dominated the swankier areas of a Roman house. But women were not meant to be publicly invisible, and domestic life does not seem to have been formally divided into male and female spaces, with gendered no-go areas. 
 
From: Mary Beard, SPQR: A History of Ancient Rome (New York: Liveright Publishing Corporation, 2015), 307. 

**Which of the following conclusions best fits the evidence Beard presents in this comparison?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Roman women had drastically superior lives than Athenian women
Roman women faced restrictions, but not as many as Athenian women
Roman women faced more restrictions than Athenian women
Roman and Athenian women had very similar lives
